chapters.title: Who really wants the job
chapters.text:
How would you feel about peeking inside the most important house in the United States? The one that looks like a white sugar cone, packed with the best of the best. The one that shapes the lives of millions, no matter how sweet the brochure makes it sound.

No more dancing around it. It is time to bring Mr. Trump out from behind the curtain.

This is a look at the presidency of Donald J. Trump, possibly the most talked about resident the White House has ever had. This is not a made up story. It is built on solid reporting and on the accounts of people who chose to share what they saw with Philip Rucker and Carol Leonnig. Settle in and watch the show.

The story is a bit like a puzzle. We will lay out each piece as we go. We will look at the President worries, the moments when justice may have been blocked, and the decisions that left a lot of people scratching their heads.

Life only means something if we take responsibility for it. Without that, it is a sham.

A quick warning: you may end up rethinking what you think you know about your country leaders. Knowing who they are matters, because they end up representing everyone.

The fights between parties matter less than the well being of the people they are supposed to serve. Republican, Democrat, or something else, we are all just human. Strip away the title and any big shot is just a person with quirks, not so different from the person walking past you on the street.

As the story unfolds, we will step inside the mind of the man who turned American diplomacy on its head, and we will try on the role for a while with Donald J. Trump.

chapters.title: Slow down before you step inside the White House
chapters.text:
The opening stretch of Donald Trump time in office was marked by a surprising lack of preparation. He cared most about loyalty, so he quickly picked Michael Flynn, a retired lieutenant general, to be his national security advisor. Flynn had already drawn criticism for his anti Islam comments and his closeness to Russia. He also had a habit of leaning on shaky facts and bold claims.

Trump did not hesitate. Flynn history did not bother him. In the days before the inauguration, the President elect did not know that Flynn was under investigation for possibly working on behalf of the Russian government and potentially betraying the United States. The center of that inquiry was a call Flynn made on December 29, 2016, to Russian Ambassador Sergei Kislyak. Flynn and the ambassador discussed the sanctions that the Obama administration had placed on Russia.

After learning this, FBI Deputy Director Andrew McCabe warned senior officials about how serious the situation was.

At the same time, Trump distrust of the so called Deep State was growing, and he was eager to clear his name from the Russia probe being run by FBI Director James Comey.

When trust breaks down and competence is low, even a great system can fall apart.

Trump pushed Comey to publicly declare him innocent. Officials who cared about the integrity of the investigations refused to go along. This stretch showed the tension between Trump and his own team, with impulsive choices colliding with the concerns of the people around him.

One dramatic moment came at the start of May 2017, when Trump suddenly fired Comey. The decision was made on the fly while his top aide was out of town. The dismissal sent shock through the political world.

In the middle of this chaos, special prosecutor Robert Mueller arrived at the White House. Deputy Attorney General Rod Rosenstein believed Mueller had what it took to run the Russia investigation. He could handle the politics with care, honesty, and a deep understanding of national security and cyber warfare. Mueller arrival made it clear how serious the situation had become and marked a turning point in the Trump presidency.

As the authors put it, in Mueller, Trump met a stubborn and untouchable opponent.

chapters.title: Walking on hot coals
chapters.text:
Trump office came under heavier scrutiny as the investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election rolled on. The spotlight fell hard on his actions, raising questions about whether he had tried to block justice.

The White House wanted to discredit Mueller and his team. Rosenstein refused to fire Mueller without a real reason, which made clear that the investigation was independent. That move highlighted how serious the moment was.

The White House did not just sit still, though. On June 21, 2017, reports surfaced that the administration was looking for ways to undermine the probe. The Wall Street Journal described attempts to discredit key figures in the investigation and to cast doubt on what the President called a witch hunt.

You can cover a leak, but the water still finds a way through.

In the middle of this political storm, claims of cover ups and lies grew louder. The administration faced accusations of hiding important details from the public, especially around contacts with foreign players.

One striking revelation was that the President had gone to unusual lengths to keep quiet about his face to face meetings with Russian leader Vladimir Putin. Withholding that kind of information from his own administration was unusual for a president. The secrecy fed speculation about what was really going on between Trump and Putin.

As the authors reported, Trump liked to brag that he shared a special bond with Putin because they were stablemates on 60 Minutes.

The evidence handed to the special counsel painted a picture of Trump office trying to control its image and to hold back or change information. Key people inside were named as part of those efforts, which deepened the intrigue.

This was a wild stretch marked by unpredictable behavior and decisions, and a lot of people were worried. The mood was full of uncertainty, and there was a real feeling that things were slipping out of control.

One fun fact: Donald Trump was the first president to push for a full audit of the Defense Department. The decision was that the audit would take several years to finish.

chapters.title: What goes around finds its way back
chapters.text:
Few things shape all of us, whether we clock in at a regular job or run a country, the way the media does. Donald Trump was a huge TV fan, especially the show Fox and Friends. He often used TV to stay updated and to gauge how people saw him. He even recorded some shows so he could scrub to the parts where they talked about him.

Why does that matter? Knowing Donald Trump, this habit shaped his decisions. Any criticism from the screen stung him and pushed him into action. TV helped him keep an eye on his image, but criticism often outweighed the advice of his experts and pushed him into snap choices.

Trump behavior was hard to predict during the investigation. He could be calm one moment and fired up the next, which made it tough for his team to know what he would do next.

Every choice has a consequence, and the only thing you can control is what you do.

Speaking of the investigation, no one really knew what Mueller was up to, because he and his team did not talk to the press. Even with that uncertainty, the White House decided to cooperate fully. They gave Mueller the documents he asked for and let him interview heavyweights in the administration.

Half a world away, Vladimir Putin won the Russian election, which impressed Trump. Trump was one of the few leaders warm toward Putin and even wanted to congratulate him personally, while other world leaders kept their distance. That raised more questions as the probe continued.

Things inside the White House did not get calmer. Michael Flynn was not the only Michael with a shady past. Trump lawyer, Michael Cohen, found himself in a tight spot when the FBI raided his office. The raid turned up evidence that in 2016, Cohen had paid one hundred thirty thousand dollars to an adult film actress, Stephanie Clifford, to keep quiet about an affair with the President that went back to 2006. To put that in context, Trump and the First Lady, Melania, had married a year earlier.

In the end, the President was harvesting the crop his habits had planted.

chapters.title: A leader in trouble
chapters.text:
Things heated up further at the Group of Seven summit in Quebec in 2018. The gathering brought together Germany chancellor Angela Merkel, France president Emmanuel Macron, and UK prime minister Theresa May, alongside other leaders, to talk and cooperate. This round, though, felt different.

The other leaders wanted Trump to stick to the standard rules of international cooperation. To him, it looked like a pile on. After some pushback, he agreed to a joint statement, but he added a strange flourish. He flicked a couple of Starburst candies across the table toward Chancellor Merkel and quipped, Here, Angela, do not say I never give you anything. That bizarre gesture showed a side of Trump diplomacy that had little in common with the usual gravity of high level talks.

You can bend the rules, but only when there is a good reason.

Inside the Trump administration, there was plenty of friction. People did not always see eye to eye, and several choices met open pushback. One flashpoint was an anonymous op ed in The New York Times on September 5, 2018, which claimed that the White House was actively working to block the President more controversial decisions. The piece threw the administration into turmoil and fed distrust. The author turned out to be Miles Taylor, a former chief of staff at the Department of Homeland Security.

The essay painted a picture of a White House in free fall, with staffers worried about Trump ability to lead. The hunt for the anonymous writer drove an even deeper wedge through the team.

Another striking moment came at the memorial service for Senator John McCain. Several administration figures showed up even though Trump had not wanted them to. It was a quiet protest against Trump combative history with McCain.

Around this period, several officials openly pushed back on orders they opposed. Defense Secretary James Mattis stepped down over Trump decision to pull troops out of Syria. Secretary of State Rex Tillerson objected to a number of the President policies.

Put together, the White House seemed to be sliding into deeper disorder. The worry was not only his questionable diplomatic calls, but his personality and how he led. Donald Trump was the kind of leader that kept people up at night.

chapters.title: Do not count on anything too soon
chapters.text:
For most of Trump term, the White House felt like a bomb with a short fuse. On December 18, 2018, a big decision took center stage. After days of heated talks, the President did something out of character. He agreed to a spending deal to keep the government funded for two months.

The concession was a tactical move to avoid a government shutdown over the holidays. Republican leaders thought a shutdown would be a political disaster, and conservative media slammed Trump for giving in to Democrats.

The wild ride did not stop there. On February 27, 2019, Michael Cohen, once a close ally of the President, admitted to lying about Trump lack of involvement in the Moscow Trump Tower project.

Cohen confession pulled back the curtain on a web of lies and a string of contacts with Russian officials aimed at pushing the project forward. The revelation shook the administration and put Democratic Congressman Elijah Cummings in charge of the House Oversight Committee probe.

Eventually, the Report on the Investigation into Russian Interference in the 2016 Presidential Election landed on the desk of the Justice Department. The four hundred forty eight page report stunned officials because Mueller team had not redacted anything. It did, however, use footnotes to flag sensitive material from grand jury proceedings. The lack of redactions meant the Justice Department had a lot of extra work to do before the report could be released to the public.

At the same time, Mueller report did not come down hard on Trump, which made the President feel bulletproof and bolder in his next moves. On July 25, 2019, he called Volodymyr Zelenskyy, the newly elected Ukrainian president. He pressured Zelenskyy into cooperating with an investigation into former Vice President Joe Biden and his son Hunter, who was accused of taking money from a Ukrainian energy company.

A false sense of safety can lead to real foolishness.

The call was read as an attempt to damage one of his political rivals. The nature and fallout of that call set off alarm bells across the administration and the wider political world.

Trump thought the investigation was behind him. His actions that day dragged him into a new fight, with more probes and more political chaos.

chapters.title: Wrapping it up
chapters.text:
The presidency of Donald J. Trump was one of a kind, in every sense. In four years, he managed to shake up both the White House and the international stage. Trump style diplomacy will end up in history books as an example of what to avoid, and it showed just how fragile politics can be in the wrong hands.

Rucker and Leonnig did a sharp job pulling back the curtain on the President paranoia and his habit of making snap decisions. All of his controversial moves are burned into the memory of the people and politicians who watched him up close.

Power is a heavy weight, and not everyone is ready to carry it. Some people are born leaders, and some pick it up because they have to. A few never grow into the role, full of naive self confidence, and that often hurts the people around them. The forty fifth President fits that last group.

The White House had plenty of dark corners, and Robert Mueller investigation lit them up, at least for the political establishment. Once again, Rucker and Leonnig were the ones who brought that fire of knowledge to the rest of us.

From immigration to foreign policy, they dug into Trump divisive decisions without hiding his habit of stretching the truth and putting himself first.

It is not easy being a president, and it is not easy being a journalist who covers one. One thing we know for sure is that running a country is a team effort between the administration and the President. In Trump case, his office was not paid enough.

A few things you can try.

Follow current events and politics to get a better feel for how leadership works in practice.

Read a range of news outlets to get a fuller picture of political figures and the issues around them.

Talk about political topics with an open mind. Listen to different points of view and keep the debate healthy.

If you read all the way to the end, well done.
